The {{nihongo|''' Umamukaiyama Kofun'''|宇摩向山古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|Kofun]], located in the Kinseicho neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Shikokuchūō, Ehime|Shikokuchūō, Ehime]] on the island of [[Shikoku]] in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. It was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2011.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/240393|title=宇摩向山古墳 |trans-title= Umamukaiyama Kofun|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2022}}</ref> It is the largest rectangular Kofun in [[Shikoku]].
==Overview== The Umamukaiyama Kofun is located on a hill with an elevation of 20 meters on the east bank of the Kinsei River that flows through the eastern part of the Uma Plain, which is roughly in the center of the [[Seto Inland Sea]] of [[Shikoku]]. The tumulus is a rectangular {{nihongo|''{{ill|Square Kofun|lt=hōfun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''|方墳|1=Square Kofun||}}, with two side-entry stone [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]]s that both open to the south. An [[archaeological excavation]] conducted from 2003 to 2009 found that the tumulus measured 46 meters from north-to-south by 70 meters east-to-west, and had a moat with a width of five meters and depth of two meters on at least the south and north sides. Burial Chamber No. 1 to the west was 10.8 meters long, with an internal room of 3.9 by 2.5 meters and a height of 2.6 meters, and is in good condition. Burial Chamber No. 2 to the east was 14.3 meters long, and its internal room was estimated to be 3.8 meters high, and is partially collapsed. The chambers were made from massive monoliths of {{ill|Schist|en|Schist|WD=Q15315|lt=crystalline schist}} weighing several hundred tons, which had been quarried to the south of the {{ill|Shikoku Mountains|en|Shikoku Mountains|WD=Q1093753|lt=Shikoku Mountains}} and brought to this location by some unknown means. From the fact that the two stone chambers are parallel and the elevation of the floor surface of both stone chambers is the same, it seems that the construction of two stone chambers. was intended from the beginning of the construction of the tumulus. The size of the monoliths and burial chambers has led the tumulus to be dubbed the "Iyo no Ishibutai" after the famous [[Ishibutai Kofun]] in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|en|Asuka, Nara|WD=Q752397|lt=Asuka, Nara}}.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The tumulus is estimated have been constructed in the early 7th century, based on the {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery}} and other {{ill|Grave goods|en|Grave goods|WD=Q1399692|lt=Grave goods}}, such as gilt bronze rings and {{ill|chalcedony|en|chalcedony|WD=Q170448|lt=chalcedony}} ''[[magatama]]'' found in Burial Chamber No1, when the door was opened in 1893 by local antiquarians. The whereabouts of most of these artifacts is now unknown.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
<gallery> Uma Mukaiyama Kofun, funkyu.jpg|Umamukaiyama Kofun Uma Mukaiyama Kofun-1, genshitsu-1.jpg|No.1 Burial Chamber, far end Uma Mukaiyama Kofun-1, sendou-2.jpg|No1 Burial Chamber, entry </gallery>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Uma Mukaiyama Kofun]][[en:Umamukaiyama Kofun]][[ja:宇摩向山古墳]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Uma Mukaiyama Kofun]][[en:Umamukaiyama Kofun]][[ja:宇摩向山古墳]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Uma Mukaiyama Kofun]][[en:Umamukaiyama Kofun]][[ja:宇摩向山古墳]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11449416]] Wikidata name: Umamukaiyama Kofun [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:15, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Kofun in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Rectangular Kofun|ja|長方形墳|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rectangular Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} | name = Umamukaiyama Kofun | native_name =宇摩向山古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = File:Uma Mukaiyama Kofun, funkyu.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Umamukaiyama Kofun, Burial Chamber No.1 | map_type = Japan Ehime Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Umamukaiyama Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = 121 | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.00673889|N|133.58483611|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_dot_label = | location = {{ill|Shikokuchuo|WD=Q838666}} | region = [[:en:Shikoku region|Shikoku region]] | type = Kofun | part_of = | length =70m | width =46m | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = early 7th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations =2003-2009 | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles ={{ill|Q80793203|WD=Q80793203}} | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|0|24.22|N|133|35|5.4|E}}}}}}
The {{nihongo|''' Umamukaiyama Kofun'''|宇摩向山古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|Kofun]], located in the Kinseicho neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Shikokuchūō, Ehime|Shikokuchūō, Ehime]] on the island of [[Shikoku]] in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. It was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2011.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/240393|title=宇摩向山古墳 |trans-title= Umamukaiyama Kofun|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2022}}</ref> It is the largest rectangular Kofun in [[Shikoku]].
==Overview== The Umamukaiyama Kofun is located on a hill with an elevation of 20 meters on the east bank of the Kinsei River that flows through the eastern part of the Uma Plain, which is roughly in the center of the [[Seto Inland Sea]] of [[Shikoku]]. The tumulus is a rectangular {{nihongo|''{{ill|Square Kofun|lt=hōfun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''|方墳|1=Square Kofun||}}, with two side-entry stone [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]]s that both open to the south. An [[archaeological excavation]] conducted from 2003 to 2009 found that the tumulus measured 46 meters from north-to-south by 70 meters east-to-west, and had a moat with a width of five meters and depth of two meters on at least the south and north sides. Burial Chamber No. 1 to the west was 10.8 meters long, with an internal room of 3.9 by 2.5 meters and a height of 2.6 meters, and is in good condition. Burial Chamber No. 2 to the east was 14.3 meters long, and its internal room was estimated to be 3.8 meters high, and is partially collapsed. The chambers were made from massive monoliths of {{ill|Schist|en|Schist|WD=Q15315|lt=crystalline schist}} weighing several hundred tons, which had been quarried to the south of the {{ill|Shikoku Mountains|en|Shikoku Mountains|WD=Q1093753|lt=Shikoku Mountains}} and brought to this location by some unknown means. From the fact that the two stone chambers are parallel and the elevation of the floor surface of both stone chambers is the same, it seems that the construction of two stone chambers. was intended from the beginning of the construction of the tumulus. The size of the monoliths and burial chambers has led the tumulus to be dubbed the "Iyo no Ishibutai" after the famous [[Ishibutai Kofun]] in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|en|Asuka, Nara|WD=Q752397|lt=Asuka, Nara}}.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The tumulus is estimated have been constructed in the early 7th century, based on the {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery}} and other {{ill|Grave goods|en|Grave goods|WD=Q1399692|lt=Grave goods}}, such as gilt bronze rings and {{ill|chalcedony|en|chalcedony|WD=Q170448|lt=chalcedony}} ''[[magatama]]'' found in Burial Chamber No1, when the door was opened in 1893 by local antiquarians. The whereabouts of most of these artifacts is now unknown.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
<gallery> Uma Mukaiyama Kofun, funkyu.jpg|Umamukaiyama Kofun Uma Mukaiyama Kofun-1, genshitsu-1.jpg|No.1 Burial Chamber, far end Uma Mukaiyama Kofun-1, sendou-2.jpg|No1 Burial Chamber, entry </gallery>
==See also== *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Ehime)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Ehime)|WD=Q6573259|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Ehime)}}

The size of the monoliths and burial chambers has led the tumulus to be dubbed the "Iyo no Ishibutai" after the famous [[Ishibutai Kofun]] in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|en|Asuka, Nara|WD=Q752397|lt=Asuka, Nara}}.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The tumulus is estimated have been constructed in the early 7th century, based on the {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery}} and other {{ill|Grave goods|en|Grave goods|WD=Q1399692|lt=Grave goods}}, such as gilt bronze rings and {{ill|chalcedony|en|chalcedony|WD=Q170448|lt=chalcedony}} ''[[magatama]]'' found in Burial Chamber No1, when the door was opened in 1893 by local antiquarians. The whereabouts of most of these artifacts is now unknown.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
<gallery> Uma Mukaiyama Kofun, funkyu.jpg|Umamukaiyama Kofun Uma Mukaiyama Kofun-1, genshitsu-1.jpg|No.1 Burial Chamber, far end Uma Mukaiyama Kofun-1, sendou-2.jpg|No1 Burial Chamber, entry </gallery>

{{Short description|Kofun in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Rectangular Kofun|ja|長方形墳|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rectangular Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} | name = Umamukaiyama Kofun | native_name =宇摩向山古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = File:Uma Mukaiyama Kofun, funkyu.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Umamukaiyama Kofun, Burial Chamber No.1 | map_type = Japan Ehime Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Umamukaiyama Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = 121 | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.00673889|N|133.58483611|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_dot_label = | location = {{ill|Shikokuchuo|WD=Q838666|1=Shikokuchūō}} | region = [[:en:Shikoku region|Shikoku region]] | type = Kofun | part_of = | length =70m | width =46m | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = early 7th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations =2003-2009 | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles ={{ill|Q80793203|WD=Q80793203|1=Rectangular Kofun}} | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|0|24.22|N|133|35|5.4|E}}}}}}
The {{nihongo|''' Umamukaiyama Kofun'''|宇摩向山古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|Kofun]], located in the Kinseicho neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Shikokuchūō, Ehime|Shikokuchūō, Ehime]] on the island of [[Shikoku]] in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. It was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2011.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/240393|title=宇摩向山古墳 |trans-title= Umamukaiyama Kofun|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2022}}</ref> It is the largest rectangular Kofun in [[Shikoku]].
==Overview== The Umamukaiyama Kofun is located on a hill with an elevation of 20 meters on the east bank of the Kinsei River that flows through the eastern part of the Uma Plain, which is roughly in the center of the [[Seto Inland Sea]] of [[Shikoku]]. The tumulus is a rectangular {{nihongo|''{{ill|Square Kofun|lt=hōfun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''|方墳|1=Square Kofun||}}, with two side-entry stone [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]]s that both open to the south. An [[archaeological excavation]] conducted from 2003 to 2009 found that the tumulus measured 46 meters from north-to-south by 70 meters east-to-west, and had a moat with a width of five meters and depth of two meters on at least the south and north sides. Burial Chamber No. 1 to the west was 10.8 meters long, with an internal room of 3.9 by 2.5 meters and a height of 2.6 meters, and is in good condition. Burial Chamber No. 2 to the east was 14.3 meters long, and its internal room was estimated to be 3.8 meters high, and is partially collapsed. The chambers were made from massive monoliths of {{ill|Schist|en|Schist|WD=Q15315|lt=crystalline schist|1=Schist}} weighing several hundred tons, which had been quarried to the south of the {{ill|Shikoku Mountains|en|Shikoku Mountains|WD=Q1093753|lt=Shikoku Mountains|1=Shikoku Mountains}} and brought to this location by some unknown means. From the fact that the two stone chambers are parallel and the elevation of the floor surface of both stone chambers is the same, it seems that the construction of two stone chambers. was intended from the beginning of the construction of the tumulus. The size of the monoliths and burial chambers has led the tumulus to be dubbed the "Iyo no Ishibutai" after the famous [[Ishibutai Kofun]] in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|en|Asuka, Nara|WD=Q752397|lt=Asuka, Nara|1=Asuka, Nara}}.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The tumulus is estimated have been constructed in the early 7th century, based on the {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery|1=Sue pottery}} and other {{ill|Grave goods|en|Grave goods|WD=Q1399692|lt=Grave goods|1=Grave goods}}, such as gilt bronze rings and {{ill|chalcedony|en|chalcedony|WD=Q170448|lt=chalcedony|1=Chalcedony}} ''[[magatama]]'' found in Burial Chamber No1, when the door was opened in 1893 by local antiquarians. The whereabouts of most of these artifacts is now unknown.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
<gallery> Uma Mukaiyama Kofun, funkyu.jpg|Umamukaiyama Kofun Uma Mukaiyama Kofun-1, genshitsu-1.jpg|No.1 Burial Chamber, far end Uma Mukaiyama Kofun-1, sendou-2.jpg|No1 Burial Chamber, entry </gallery>

{{Short description|Kofun in Japan}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Rectangular Kofun|ja|長方形墳|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rectangular Kofun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} | name = Umamukaiyama Kofun | native_name =宇摩向山古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = File:Uma Mukaiyama Kofun, funkyu.jpg | image_size = | alt = | caption = Umamukaiyama Kofun, Burial Chamber No.1 | map_type = Japan Ehime Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Umamukaiyama Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = 121 | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{Coord|34.00673889|N|133.58483611|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_dot_label = | location = {{ill|Shikokuchuo|WD=Q838666|1=Shikokuchūō}} | region = [[:en:Shikoku region|Shikoku region]] | type = Kofun | part_of = | length =70m | width =46m | area = | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = early 7th century | abandoned = | epochs =[[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations =2003-2009 | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles ={{ill|Q80793203|WD=Q80793203|1=Rectangular Kofun}} | architectural_details = |notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} {{maplink2|type=point|frame=yes|plain=yes|zoom=12|frame-align=center|frame-width=200|coord={{coord|34|0|24.22|N|133|35|5.4|E}}}}}}
The {{nihongo|''' Umamukaiyama Kofun'''|宇摩向山古墳|}} is a [[Kofun period]] [[kofun|Kofun]], located in the Kinseicho neighborhood of the city of [[:en:Shikokuchūō, Ehime|Shikokuchūō, Ehime]] on the island of [[Shikoku]] in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. It was designated a [[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]] in 2011.<ref name="Bunka1">{{cite web |url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/heritages/detail/240393|title=宇摩向山古墳 |trans-title= Umamukaiyama Kofun|language=Japanese |publisher=[[Agency for Cultural Affairs]] |accessdate=August 20, 2022}}</ref> It is the largest rectangular Kofun in [[Shikoku]].
==Overview== The Umamukaiyama Kofun is located on a hill with an elevation of 20 meters on the east bank of the Kinsei River that flows through the eastern part of the Uma Plain, which is roughly in the center of the [[Seto Inland Sea]] of [[Shikoku]]. The tumulus is a rectangular {{nihongo|''{{ill|Square Kofun|lt=hōfun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''|方墳|1=Square Kofun||}}, with two side-entry stone [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]]s that both open to the south. An [[archaeological excavation]] conducted from 2003 to 2009 found that the tumulus measured 46 meters from north-to-south by 70 meters east-to-west, and had a moat with a width of five meters and depth of two meters on at least the south and north sides. Burial Chamber No. 1 to the west was 10.8 meters long, with an internal room of 3.9 by 2.5 meters and a height of 2.6 meters, and is in good condition. Burial Chamber No. 2 to the east was 14.3 meters long, and its internal room was estimated to be 3.8 meters high, and is partially collapsed. The chambers were made from massive monoliths of {{ill|Schist|en|Schist|WD=Q15315|lt=crystalline schist|1=Schist}} weighing several hundred tons, which had been quarried to the south of the {{ill|Shikoku Mountains|en|Shikoku Mountains|WD=Q1093753|lt=Shikoku Mountains|1=Shikoku Mountains}} and brought to this location by some unknown means. From the fact that the two stone chambers are parallel and the elevation of the floor surface of both stone chambers is the same, it seems that the construction of two stone chambers. was intended from the beginning of the construction of the tumulus. The size of the monoliths and burial chambers has led the tumulus to be dubbed the "Iyo no Ishibutai" after the famous [[Ishibutai Kofun]] in {{ill|Asuka, Nara|en|Asuka, Nara|WD=Q752397|lt=Asuka, Nara|1=Asuka, Nara}}.<ref name="Shiseki">{{cite book |last1=Isomura |first1=Yukio |last2=Sakai |first2=Hideya |title=(国指定史跡事典) National Historic Site Encyclopedia |date=2012 |publisher=学生社 |isbn=4311750404}}{{in lang|ja}}</ref>
The tumulus is estimated have been constructed in the early 7th century, based on the {{ill|Sue pottery|en|Sue pottery|WD=Q7634351|lt=Sue pottery|1=Sue pottery}} and other {{ill|Grave goods|en|Grave goods|WD=Q1399692|lt=Grave goods|1=Grave goods}}, such as gilt bronze rings and {{ill|chalcedony|en|chalcedony|WD=Q170448|lt=chalcedony|1=Chalcedony}} ''[[magatama]]'' found in Burial Chamber No1, when the door was opened in 1893 by local antiquarians. The whereabouts of most of these artifacts is now unknown.<ref name="Shiseki"/>
<gallery> Uma Mukaiyama Kofun, funkyu.jpg|Umamukaiyama Kofun Uma Mukaiyama Kofun-1, genshitsu-1.jpg|No.1 Burial Chamber, far end Uma Mukaiyama Kofun-1, sendou-2.jpg|No1 Burial Chamber, entry </gallery>
==See also== *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Ehime)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Ehime)|WD=Q6573259|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Ehime)|1=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Ehime)}}
==References== {{reflist}}
==External links== {{Commonscat|Uma Mukaiyama Kofun}} *[http://bunka.shikokuchuo.or.jp/mky/mky.htm Shikokuchūō City Board of Education Cultural Promotion Division "Cultural Assets of Shikokuchūō City"] {{in lang|ja}}
